title: n8n Workflow Automation Platform Critical RCE CVE-2026-21858 (CVSS 10.0) "Ni8mare": Content-Type Confusion in Form Webhook Enables Unauthenticated Full System Compromise — 100,000 Exposed Instances, PoC Published, CISA KEV Linked Chain

timeline.event: Vulnerability discovered and privately reported to n8n team
n8n releases patched version 1.121.0 fixing the Content-Type confusion flaw
Public disclosure: Orca Security, Cyera Research, and NVD publish technical details; PoC released approximately 9 hours after disclosure
BleepingComputer, Horizon3.ai, Picus Security publish follow-up analysis; multiple GitHub PoC exploits published
Rapid7, Aikido, Censys confirm approximately 100,000 internet-exposed n8n instances; active exploitation confirmed by VulnCheck canaries
Australian Cyber Security Centre, Canadian CCCS, and other government agencies publish advisories
CISA adds related chained vulnerability CVE-2025-68613 (n8n expression injection) to KEV catalog
VulnCheck publishes research noting CVE-2026-21858 itself should be in KEV due to confirmed exploitation; daily exploitation attempts tracked by Shadowserver and GreyNoise

simple_summary.what_happened: A maximum-severity vulnerability (CVSS 10.0) nicknamed 'Ni8mare' was discovered in n8n, a popular open-source workflow automation platform widely used by developers and businesses to connect apps and automate tasks. The flaw exists in how n8n handles file uploads through its Form Webhook feature. By sending a specially crafted HTTP request with a manipulated Content-Type header, an unauthenticated attacker on the internet can trick n8n into exposing its internal database file — which contains admin credentials. With those credentials, the attacker gains full admin access and can run any command on the server hosting n8n. Exploitation is easy and a public proof-of-concept has been available since January 2026. Attacks are ongoing, and CISA placed a related n8n vulnerability on its KEV list in March 2026.
simple_summary.who_is_affected: Organizations running self-hosted n8n workflow automation instances on versions 1.65.0 through 1.120.4. Approximately 100,000 n8n instances are publicly accessible on the internet (confirmed by Censys with 26,500+ exposed instances). The vulnerability does NOT affect n8n's managed cloud offering. Industries commonly using self-hosted n8n include technology companies, SaaS businesses, marketing automation teams, and DevOps/platform engineering teams.
simple_summary.what_attackers_do: Step 1: Attacker identifies an internet-exposed n8n instance that has a Form Webhook workflow configured (very common in automation setups). Step 2: Attacker sends an HTTP POST request to the Form Webhook endpoint with a manipulated Content-Type header (e.g., application/json instead of multipart/form-data), tricking n8n's upload handler. Step 3: The confused parser copies attacker-controlled file path references to persistent storage, allowing the attacker to read arbitrary files on the server — including /home/node/.n8n/database.sqlite. Step 4: Attacker downloads the SQLite database and extracts the administrator's hashed credentials and the encryption key from /home/node/.n8n/config. Step 5: Using extracted secrets, attacker forges a valid admin authentication token, gains full administrative access without knowing the original password, creates a malicious n8n workflow using the 'Execute Command' node, and runs arbitrary operating system commands achieving full server compromise.
what_you_should_do: Upgrade n8n to version 1.121.0 or later immediately — this is the only complete fix
If immediate upgrade is not possible, block public internet access to n8n Form Webhook endpoints as an emergency measure
Review n8n access logs for suspicious POST requests to webhook/form paths with unusual Content-Type headers since January 7, 2026
Rotate all credentials and API keys stored in n8n workflows if you cannot confirm your instance was not compromised
Move n8n behind VPN or restrict access to known IP addresses until patching is complete
simple_summary.why_this_is_serious: A CVSS 10.0 score means this vulnerability is as severe as possible: no authentication, no user interaction, no complex conditions — any attacker on the internet can fully compromise your n8n server in minutes using publicly available exploit code. n8n workflows typically store sensitive credentials for third-party services (Slack, AWS, databases, payment processors, CRMs) that get stolen along with server access. CISA's KEV listing of a related n8n flaw signals active attacks on real organizations. With 100,000+ exposed instances and PoC code freely available on GitHub, automated exploitation is straightforward and ongoing.

tactics.description: Attackers exploit the unauthenticated Content-Type confusion vulnerability in n8n's publicly accessible Form Webhook endpoint to gain initial foothold on the server.
After exploiting the file read vulnerability, attackers download the SQLite database (/home/node/.n8n/database.sqlite) to extract admin credentials and the encryption key from /home/node/.n8n/config.
Using extracted encryption secrets, attackers forge a valid admin JWT session token to authenticate as administrator without triggering authentication failure alerts.
Attackers create malicious n8n workflows using the built-in 'Execute Command' node to run arbitrary operating system commands on the host server as the n8n process user.
n8n workflows routinely store credentials for integrated services (cloud providers, databases, SaaS APIs). Attackers extract all stored integration credentials from the n8n database.

detection_guidance.summary: Detection focuses on monitoring Form Webhook HTTP traffic for Content-Type manipulation, alerting on unusual file access patterns on n8n servers, and detecting admin sessions that were not initiated via the standard login flow. No public IOCs (IP addresses, hashes) have been published as exploitation is opportunistic and distributed.

log_sources.description: Monitor n8n logs for POST requests to /webhook/ and /form/ paths with unexpected Content-Type headers (anything other than multipart/form-data for form endpoints)
Deploy WAF rules to detect and block requests with Content-Type header mismatches targeting n8n form webhook endpoints
Monitor for read access to /home/node/.n8n/database.sqlite and /home/node/.n8n/config from unexpected processes or users
Monitor for unexpected command execution spawned from the n8n Node.js process, indicating abuse of the Execute Command workflow node

what_to_look_for.indicator: POST requests to Form Webhook with non-multipart Content-Type
what_to_look_for.description: Exploitation sends a POST to /webhook-test/ or /form/ endpoint with application/json or other non-multipart Content-Type header while the body contains file path references
what_to_look_for.log_field: request_method='POST' AND url_path CONTAINS '/form/' AND content_type NOT CONTAINS 'multipart/form-data'

what_to_look_for.description: Successful exploitation results in reading of the n8n SQLite database outside of normal n8n startup or scheduled backup windows
Admin sessions created via forged JWT tokens will originate from attacker IP addresses not seen in previous login history
what_to_look_for.log_field: event_type='admin_login' AND source_ip NOT IN known_admin_ips

what_to_look_for.indicator: Execute Command workflow node triggered by unknown workflow
what_to_look_for.description: Attackers create new workflows containing Execute Command nodes; monitor for execution of system commands via n8n workflow executor

immediate_actions.action: Upgrade n8n to version 1.121.0 or later
immediate_actions.description: This is the only complete fix. Update n8n via npm or your container deployment. The patch corrects Content-Type validation in Form Webhook handling to prevent the file path manipulation attack.
immediate_actions.command:
# npm global install
npm update -g n8n
# Docker
docker pull n8nio/n8n:latest
docker compose pull && docker compose up -d

immediate_actions.action: Block public internet access to Form Webhook endpoints
immediate_actions.description: If patching is delayed, immediately restrict internet access to n8n's webhook endpoints. Place n8n behind a VPN or restrict to known IP allowlist using your WAF or firewall.

immediate_actions.action: Check for compromise indicators in access logs
immediate_actions.description: Search n8n and web server logs for POST requests to form webhook paths with unusual Content-Type headers. Also check for unexpected admin logins and new workflows created via API, especially any containing 'executeCommand' nodes.
immediate_actions.command:
# Search nginx/apache logs for suspicious webhook requests
grep -E 'POST.*/form/.*Content-Type.*application/json' /var/log/nginx/access.log

immediate_actions.action: Rotate all credentials stored in n8n workflows
immediate_actions.description: If compromise cannot be ruled out, rotate all API keys, passwords, and tokens stored in n8n credentials. This includes cloud provider credentials (AWS, GCP, Azure), database passwords, SaaS API tokens, and any other credentials managed by n8n workflows.

immediate_actions.action: Enable n8n user management and disable public form webhooks
immediate_actions.description: If your n8n use case allows, enable user authentication on the n8n instance and disable public-facing Form Webhook nodes in all workflows until the patch is applied.
